source_documents: https://www.rbi.org.in (Master Directions on Frauds - Classification & Reporting, incl. Early Warning Signals)
category: Credit fraud - inflated, forged, or non-existent collateral
risk_signals: Collateral valued well above independent market benchmarks
A collusive or related-party valuer repeatedly used for the same borrower
Collateral that is re-pledged elsewhere, encumbered, or does not physically exist
analyst_reasoning: Inflating collateral value lets a borrower draw more credit than the security can cover, so the lender is under-secured from day one. The fraud lives in the valuation and title chain: a captive valuer, an above-market figure, or re-pledged/phantom assets that unravel only at enforcement.
investigation_steps: Obtain an independent valuation and compare against the sanction valuation and market data
Check the valuer's independence and whether the same valuer recurs across the borrower's files
Verify title, existence, and prior charges/encumbrances on the collateral
decision: Re-margin or recall the facility, file a fraud report, and blacklist a colluding valuer.
outcome: Over-valued or phantom collateral produces large loss-given-default at enforcement; independent valuation is the key control.
lessons_learned: Valuation well above market plus a captive valuer is the core collateral-fraud signal
Title and encumbrance verification catches re-pledged or non-existent security
Valuer independence is a control, not a formality
